region_of_origin: Coastal waters worldwide; widely consumed in Southeast Asia, North America, and East Asia

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers crab (raw) as a broad crustacean category; no species-specific code for Portunus pelagicus or Callinectes sapidus exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. A0CJW is the closest defensible parent anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw blue crab is approximately 120 g of edible meat, equivalent to the yield from one medium whole crab.

nutrient_source.title: Blue crab, raw — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macros (energy 87 kcal, protein 18.06 g, fat 1.08 g, carbohydrate 0.04 g, sodium 293 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, potassium 329 mg, calcium 89 mg, iron 0.74 mg, cholesterol 78 mg) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrients (saturated fat 0.23 g, sugar 0.0 g, vitamin B12 9.0 mcg, zinc 3.54 mg, phosphorus 259 mg, magnesium 34 mg, selenium 36.4 mcg, water 79.0 g) cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15139 (Blue crab, raw). Values are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Blue crab, raw contains only 0.04 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI testing is performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 293 mg/100 g (borderline) offset by high potassium 329 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Raw blue crab has borderline sodium at 293 mg/100 g, but its high potassium content (329 mg/100 g) provides a favourable Na:K ratio; hypertensive individuals should consume it steamed or boiled without added salt and strictly avoid high-sodium preparations such as chilli crab sauce, black bean sauce, or salted preparations that can raise sodium intake substantially.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.04 g/100 g), zero sugar, high protein (18.06 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blue crab is an excellent protein source for people with type 2 diabetes, with virtually zero carbohydrate and no glycaemic impact; individuals should avoid sugary or starchy sauces such as chilli crab sauce or sweet black bean sauce, which significantly raise the glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.08 g/100 g), low saturated fat (0.23 g/100 g), omega-3 fatty acids present, cholesterol 78 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Blue crab is a heart-healthy protein with low saturated fat, beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), and moderate cholesterol (78 mg/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease can include it regularly as part of a balanced diet, prepared by steaming or boiling, while avoiding deep-fried or high-sodium sauce preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) reduce joint inflammation; low saturated fat; calcium 89 mg/100 g supports bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Blue crab provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and calcium that benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients; avoid deep-frying (which generates AGEs worsening joint inflammation) and high-sodium sauces that can exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blue crab (raw) is an uncooked crustacean belonging to the family Portunidae, encompassing species such as Portunus pelagicus (blue swimmer crab, widely consumed in Southeast Asia and Australia) and Callinectes sapidus (Atlantic blue crab, prominent in North American cuisine). The raw meat is tender, moist, and naturally sweet with a delicate briny flavour. The shell is characteristically blue-green before cooking, turning orange-red upon heat exposure. Per 100 g of edible raw meat, blue crab delivers 87 kcal, 18.06 g of high-quality complete protein, only 1.08 g of total fat (of which 0.23 g is saturated), and a negligible 0.04 g of carbohydrate, making it one of the leanest and most protein-dense seafood options available. It is an exceptional source of vitamin B12 (9.0 mcg/100 g, covering over 375% of the adult RDA), zinc (3.54 mg), selenium (36.4 mcg), phosphorus (259 mg), and potassium (329 mg). Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) are present and contribute anti-inflammatory benefits. Sodium is 293 mg/100 g in the raw state, which is borderline but offset by the high potassium content. Cholesterol is moderate at 78 mg/100 g. Clinically, blue crab is suitable for type 2 diabetes management (negligible carbohydrate, zero glycaemic impact), cardiovascular health (low saturated fat, omega-3 present), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory omega-3, calcium for bone support). Hypertensive individuals should prefer steamed or boiled preparations without added salt. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, blue crab is commonly prepared as chilli crab, black pepper crab, or steamed crab; the raw ingredient itself is nutritionally excellent, but sauce-based preparations significantly alter the sodium, sugar, and calorie profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in blue crab?
Is blue crab good for diabetes?
Is blue crab high in cholesterol?
Is blue crab high in protein?
Can I eat blue crab if I have high blood pressure?
What vitamins are in blue crab?
Is blue crab good for heart health?
What is the difference between blue swimmer crab and Atlantic blue crab?

confidence_reason: Primary macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15139 (Blue crab, raw), which is an exact species-level match. Both approved sources are consistent. No estimation or analogy was required for core nutritional values.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.04 g (not zero); this is a confirmed trace amount per HPB and USDA SR Legacy — not naturally zero but nutritionally negligible. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy (no detectable sugars in raw blue crab). Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as crustaceans contain no plant-based dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/absent in raw crustacean per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin_d_mcg and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry, and USDA SR Legacy NDB 15139 does not provide vitamin D or vitamin E values for blue crab raw. GI is not applicable: blue crab contains only 0.04 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index testing is not performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content, and glycemic_index is set to null per the Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had an internal contradiction (suitable: false but reason described anti-inflammatory benefits); this has been corrected to reflect the food's genuine anti-inflammatory profile with appropriate cautions. Halal status: blue crab is generally considered halal under mainstream Sunni jurisprudence; however, some schools differ — consumers should verify with their religious authority. Kosher: crab is not kosher (lacks fins and scales). Ontology: FoodEx2 code A0CJW is used as the closest parent broad crustacean anchor; no species-specific FoodEx2 code for Portunus pelagicus or Callinectes sapidus exists in the public catalogue; foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent'.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Changed foodex2_match_type from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent', which is one of the three allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] per the validator. Updated foodex2_gap_note accordingly. This resolves the g5_ontology_honesty hard gate failure.

Iteration 3: Confirmed glycemic_index remains null (not a numeric value) per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. The validator flagged 'glycemic_index must be numeric' but the Veritas critical rule explicitly states null must be used for foods with negligible carbohydrates and that setting glycemic_index=0 will cause a q4 gate failure. The null value is correct and compliant with the Veritas GI critical rule. gi_category 'not_applicable' and gi_source documentation are retained as correct.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology codes and gap notes from iteration 1 draft.

Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, preserving HPB-sourced macros, adding USDA SR Legacy cross-reference for micronutrients, correcting RA health suitability contradiction, and completing all mandatory sections.
